author: @adrianfrench7842
text:
There was a heat driven car in the '80s powered by molten wax, and caustic soda steam driven rail cars before that. 
As I understand it the energy density of metal oxide frameworks (for thermal storage) can equal or surpass current lithium batteries. 
Maybe we should look at fully thermal powered cars instead of converting electricity to heat to kinetic energy.
Hybrid photovoltaic/thermal panels only cost about 10% more than standard PV panels with the same PV cells and can collect more than 3 times as much thermal energy as their electric output. The biggest problem with thermal engines has typically been that they require very high temperatures to operate efficiently and convenient green sources of heat just aren't hot enough, but heat driven heat pumps have now achieved COP greater than 1 and desiccant materials can be regenerated at lower temperatures by use of a partial vacuum, so it should be possible to raise thermal energy from solar to workable temperatures for recharging MOF thermal storage without huge efficiency issues.

author: @SheplerStudios
text:
I learn so much from your channel Robert and greatly appreciate your sharing and instruction, much appreciated.

Agree that people are far too focused on efficiency vs. cost/kWh. Stirlings are so relatively easy and cheap to manufacture that simply scaling them in size and number is a far easier solution to increased power production vs. more sophisticated technology relient complex supply chains and geopolitics.

The ability to build and operate simply systems, particularly those originating from repurposed resources will become increasingly important as Climate Change and it's effects on global economies reduces availability and raises costs of high tech materials and manufacturing.

Stirling engines are perfect in this regard in that anyone with access to a junkyard, metal lathe and a welder can pretty much build a pump, electrical generator, etc. for next to nothing.

text: I think you might be confusing 'efficiency relative to carnot efficiency' with 'efficiency'. Sterling engines can do pretty well on the former; but on the whole, converting electricity first to heat is a pretty aweful idea. Especially if your goal is to avoid rare earths, which as many of Tesla's cars demonstrate, can be achieved perfectly well with good old induction motors with modern electronic control.

author: @peterdkay

replies.author: @RusselwithoneL
replies.text:
I googled "most efficient sterling engine and without even having to scroll down the page I got this information:


"One of the most efficient Stirling engines ever made was the MOD II automotive engine, produced in the 1980′s. It reached a peak thermal efficiency of 38.5%. Compare this to a modern spark-ignition (gasoline) engine, which has a peak efficiency of 20-25%."


In a scenario where energy is abundant but rare earth metal are not then this would make sense. I do believe the video was about the scarcity of rare earth metals and not energy. In a scenario where the worlds energy needs are solved then 38.5% efficiency or even 10% gets to sit at the table for a discussion.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate - I am sorry but that's just not right I am afraid. Plus what you are looking at is efficiency and really cost per kWh is also a very interesting metric and is why there are so many solutions being explored

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely mate

replies.author: @zazugee
replies.text:
Stirling engine's efficiency is more to do with the temperature differential than anything else
so giving it a value like 10% doesnt mean anything, there are different forms and different uses
like some new linear Stirling that use hydrogen and helium as a gas and not air
the 10% are the ones that use low temperature differentials and thus Carnot cycle says theoretical efficiency is low
the reason why Stirling arnt used for higher thermal differentials have more to do with the low power density than anything else
this is why gas turbines are more popular

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Stirling cycle for heat pump is more interesting for heating and cooling. No greenhouse or  ozone depleting gases.  Electricity to transmit energy and an electric motor to provide mechanical work to a stirling heat pump.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe - but really - sometimes you need to just explore and have a bit of fun

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes! Sometimes when  I get an idea, I can't think of anything else untill I just do that idea. 👍

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @daviddavids2884  i understand your saying insteresting does not always mean useful or practical.  And, as for all heat pumps and heat engine, including the stirling cycle, they have a limited surface area that resulting in pratical limits with heat dissipation.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@daviddavids2884 I am still in the design phase of building an alpha stirling solar heat pump with two rebuilt pistons and cylinders from used vehicle shock absorbers. 
My inspiration for using shock absorbers for a stirling heat pump. https://youtu.be/hnXLNO7DkdY
Except, I'll be using different  linkage system to drive the pistons for better balanced weight.

I was inspired by this guy's use of  many parallel gas channels to increase the  heat exchanger surface area https://youtu.be/bQ1wOIsrEUA

My goal is a heating coefficient of performance (COP) of hopefully 4 or better.   That is, take about 250 watts of power to spin an electric motor, and get about about 1kws of heat energy per second from pumping 750 watts seconds  from 2 sq meters of solar thermal heat collectors assuming 50% or less collection efficiency. Using a heat exchange fluid, I can pump solar heated fluid through a micro channel heat exchanger providing heat to the stirling heat pump.  Then the stirling heat pump can move the heat to the other side  and under compression give up  its higher heat to another micro channel fluid heat exchanger. Next pump that higher temperature fluid through fluid through a used automotive radiator and fan, dumping the heat into my house.

A stirling heat cycle engine or heat pump power rating is somewhat proportional to the compression in the gas. 

I believe my goal is practically achievable and using renewable energy for the heat source and reusing parts that would have been thrown away may help to keep this economically pratical.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
First thing.  Mary Christmas!

Elecric Motors without magnets:
Induction motors, reluctance motors, universal shunt and serries wound motors, all have no magnets. And I'm pretty sure they are more energy efficent than a using a stirling to generate reciprocating motion to inturn make rotational motion.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you might want to have a read at the NASA research on this mate

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  certainly the stirling engine has a theoretically high carnot efficiency.   Heat engine efficiency is governed by carnot efficacy that is in turn limited by the  temperature differential, and is different than its thermodynamic efficiency.

Many of NASA's papers  have a stirling heat engine eject its waste  heat to the cold depths of space. But here on earth,  if we say the cold side of a heat engine is 21c and the hot side is 400c then the carnot or max theoretical efficency  is 56.3%

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I love stirling engines. I have been researching them papers intently for awhile now, including NASA's stirling heat pump. 

I am still in the design phase of building an alpha stirling solar heat pump with two rebuilt pistons and cylinders from used vehicle shock absorbers. 
My inspiration for using shock absorbers for a stirling heat pump. https://youtu.be/hnXLNO7DkdY
Except, I'll be using different  linkage system to drive the pistons for better balanced weight.

I was inspired by this guy's use of  many parallel gas channels to increase the heat exchanger surface area. https://youtu.be/bQ1wOIsrEUA

My goal is a heating coefficient of performance (COP) of hopefully 4 or better.   That is, take about 250 watts of power to spin an electric motor, and get about about 1kws of heat energy per second from pumping 750 watts seconds  from 2 sq meters of solar thermal heat collectors assuming 50% or less collection efficiency. Using a heat exchange fluid, I can pump solar heated fluid through a micro channel heat exchanger providing heat to the stirling heat pump.  Then the stirling heat pump can move the heat to the other side  and under compression give up  its higher heat to another micro channel fluid heat exchanger. Next pump that higher temperature fluid through fluid through a used automotive radiator and fan, dumping the heat into my house.

author: @NeoShameMan
text:
Another non jocke idea: finding a way to translate light or any electromagnetic radiation into the radiowave ranges. To be more precise, any electromagnetic range absorbed by the atmosphere into ranges to which atmosphere is transparent. That mean that you would capture energy around and radiate it back to space. This will probably create a local cooling, and maybe enough to move a stirling engine or it least cool a room.

I was wondering if there was a way to create a passive sandwiches of materials that would do that.

author: @zattan2002
text: What if we use a Fresnel lens in order to make a Stirling engine turn? If we use small lens and engine, the volume of the system could fit in a 10x10x10cm cube, or less. Then, we could make a matrix of such small solar engines and connect them all together mechanicaly. The result would be a pannel that could be connected to an alternator or any turning tool. Why not?

replies.author: @zattan2002
replies.text: In the other way, we could use one big engine and one big parabolic reflector in order to make electricity. Even if the efficiency is not the best, the technology level and the costs are pretty low, which is good.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: why not indeed mate - give it a go

replies.text:

The output from the Stirling engine runs a small generator

author: @MartinPHellwig
text: The concept is I think sound, however to make something a little more powerful you would need the configuration of a 4 cylinder double acting SE. Personally I think that going for an  BLDC motor and swapping the permanent magnets out for electro magnets fed via a separate induction/rectifier circuit is the way to go, no rare earth materials and ridiculously powerful for its size/weight.

author: @JamesBiggar
text: I don't know, bud. Manufacturing electric motors does have an environmental impact, but so does manufacturing an ICE or a stirling engine, not to mention maintenance. There are ways around using rare earth materials - which, despite the name, are plentiful but controlled by a few. An axial flux wind turbine, for example, commonly uses neodymiums. But some builders will opt for slightly larger ferrite mags. 50% of the reason I've started switching to EV's is due to what it takes to maintain an ICE and all of the systems designed around it just to get it to work. I'm so sick of my old truck, I don't even drive it anymore for fear of breaking down and having to fix something. I see the same issue with stirling engines, hybrids, HHO, etc. Lots of systems and moving parts required. Metal parts. Composite parts, that will need to be replaced much sooner than the coils or mags in a permanent magnet motor. When someone claims something is dirty, I ask, compared to what? I fear you might be going in the wrong direction with this. I would first determine what the environmental benefit would be, exactly. I'm talking data, not just speculation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure mate - it's just a fun idea is all - who knows where fun ideas will lead
